tweets.text: If people have good ideas for how to serve one another, GDP-per-person everywhere in the world is about to 10x

thread_tweets.text: If people have good ideas for how to serve one another, GDP-per-person everywhere in the world is about to 10x
thread_tweets.full_text: If people have good ideas for how to serve one another, GDP-per-person everywhere in the world is about to 10x

thread_tweets.text: AI turns one person with a good idea into the team, the distribution, and the capital efficiency of a whole company. The cost of serving another human being well will be 0. That's the 10x. It's going to multiply every single person who wants to be multiplied.
thread_tweets.full_text: AI turns one person with a good idea into the team, the distribution, and the capital efficiency of a whole company. The cost of serving another human being well will be 0. That's the 10x. It's going to multiply every single person who wants to be multiplied.

thread_tweets.text: For the entire industrial era you could only serve at scale by being big. A great idea for helping people died unless you could raise money, hire a team, and buy distribution. The idea was never the bottleneck. The machinery around it was.
thread_tweets.full_text: For the entire industrial era you could only serve at scale by being big. A great idea for helping people died unless you could raise money, hire a team, and buy distribution. The idea was never the bottleneck. The machinery around it was.

thread_tweets.text: GDP per person is just a measure of how much value we create for each other. It's been capped for one boring reason: serving people well used to require capital, headcount, and distribution almost nobody had. AI removes all three.
thread_tweets.full_text: GDP per person is just a measure of how much value we create for each other. It's been capped for one boring reason: serving people well used to require capital, headcount, and distribution almost nobody had. AI removes all three.

thread_tweets.text:
So the real constraint on human wealth was never resources. It was good ideas for how to serve one another, and the leverage to act on them. We just deleted the leverage constraint for everybody. 

Now it's only the ideas. Go have them, and then build them. It's your time.
thread_tweets.full_text:
So the real constraint on human wealth was never resources. It was good ideas for how to serve one another, and the leverage to act on them. We just deleted the leverage constraint for everybody. 

Now it's only the ideas. Go have them, and then build them. It's your time.

thread_tweets.text: I'm in Osaka now, and it's clear: Japan already ran the experiment on the ceiling. Thirty years of zero growth and it built the best trains, service, and craft on earth. When you can't compete on more, you compete on better. Better AND more at the same time.
thread_tweets.full_text: I'm in Osaka now, and it's clear: Japan already ran the experiment on the ceiling. Thirty years of zero growth and it built the best trains, service, and craft on earth. When you can't compete on more, you compete on better. Better AND more at the same time.

tweets.full_text:
If people have good ideas for how to serve one another, GDP-per-person everywhere in the world is about to 10x

AI turns one person with a good idea into the team, the distribution, and the capital efficiency of a whole company. The cost of serving another human being well will be 0. That's the 10x. It's going to multiply every single person who wants to be multiplied.

For the entire industrial era you could only serve at scale by being big. A great idea for helping people died unless you could raise money, hire a team, and buy distribution. The idea was never the bottleneck. The machinery around it was.

GDP per person is just a measure of how much value we create for each other. It's been capped for one boring reason: serving people well used to require capital, headcount, and distribution almost nobody had. AI removes all three.

So the real constraint on human wealth was never resources. It was good ideas for how to serve one another, and the leverage to act on them. We just deleted the leverage constraint for everybody. 

Now it's only the ideas. Go have them, and then build them. It's your time.

I'm in Osaka now, and it's clear: Japan already ran the experiment on the ceiling. Thirty years of zero growth and it built the best trains, service, and craft on earth. When you can't compete on more, you compete on better. Better AND more at the same time.

@tiltegog Whatever bro

@hmoneymafia society 

have you tried living in it?

@tiltegog Yeah I know, I'm just deflecting the cringe

I'd rather be cringe and right though

tweets.text:
A data center isn't just compute

It's a financing vehicle that pulls power generation, grid, chips, and construction along with it. One buildout drags a dozen adjacent industries forward.

We have to keep building.
tweets.quoted_tweet_text:
🇺🇸 AMERICAN DATA CENTERS 🇺🇸

Excellent piece in FT by my friend @joshzoff arguing that the US can't treat data centers like we treated rare earths, and that data centers are a great way to finance development of a bunch of other advanced technologies (strong agree). https://t.co/GvFaZ252Gn
tweets.full_text:
A data center isn't just compute

It's a financing vehicle that pulls power generation, grid, chips, and construction along with it. One buildout drags a dozen adjacent industries forward.

We have to keep building.

tweets.text:
Based on that performance (and how poor Brazil were) a far harder quarter final opposition now awaits.

Maybe the best game of the world cup coming up next, certainly one of the most anticipated.

Let's hope it doesn't disappoint and we can settle the nerves and the atmosphere enough to progress.

A very tough ask ahead, the scenes in Mexico will be once in a lifetime, over a million predicted out on the streets.

Good luck.

tweets.text:
Came across an interesting report from SVRC Research called "State of Robotics 2026", published in April. 

Which listed:

1. Figure AI 
2. Agility Robotics $CCXI
3. Apptronik 
4. $TSLA 
5. Boston Dynamics
6. Physical Intelligence 
7. 1X Technologies
8. $AMZN Robotics
9. Covariant
10. Skild AI 

As the National Champions of the United States robotics program. 

"The United States leads the world in where robotics is heading: Fundation models, OpenAI-style scaling laws applied to action, autonomous vehicles. 

While losing the race on where robotics is shipping today." 

Then it frames:

1. Rare Earths Exposure: from Neodymium for motors to samarium-cobalt for high-temp applications as a critical vulnerability.

2. Actuator dependency. Series elastic actuators, quasi-direct-drive motors, and precision reducers overwhelmingly sourced from Japan, Germany, and China

As one of the main vulnerabilities alongside Manufacturing velocity/data collection cost/regulations. Then their take was: 

"With at least six well-funded US humanoid companies competing for a market still in early formation, we expect at least two significant consolidation events (acquisition or merger) in 2027". 

With Logistics / E-commerce (like $AMZN / $FDX) and Automotive from $GM to $FORD as being the immediate top use cases for deployment.

I think it's just interesting to see a lot of my points I've been talking about reiterated by research firms. Regardless, I do think it's going to be a major frontier race between the US and China. 

Agility Robotics (which I own), Tesla, Figure, and Apptronik as leaders representing the USA. Competing against Unitree, AGIbot, Ubtech, and others in China.

thread_tweets.text:
Yep, $AMZN looks like the clear winner in physical AI shift. 

I think the Amazon ecosystem... is probably going to the catalyst for robotics players compared to others in the Google/Meta ecosystems. 

I kinda see parallels to the $GOOGL TPU effect on suppliers like $LITE or Mediatek, but for robotics... like Agility. Still early though.

tweets.text:
CD was a technology invented around 1985, hit its mainstream peak in 2000s and became obsolete before 2010

So 20-25 years overall

Bitcoin is a distributed network - an information technology that exists for 16 years since its inception, you can say its "mainstream" for last 2-3 years since we have ETFs

20 years is nothing in grand scheme of things, and concluding that Bitcoin is some rock solid thousand year old institution now is quite hubristic

thread_tweets.text:
"But you don't get it, there is 1 trillion USD already in Bitcoin, there are ETFs, pension funds are in, the institutions have vested interest to make it work"

Well SpaceX is 2T, NVDA 4T

As far as big guys are concerned they care only as long as it brings them fees and clients, when their clients stop showing appetite, the priorities will change fast

Bitcoin is still tech and tech world can moves on fast

Sure Bitcoin's decentralisation and resistance to change is one of its desired properties but if its be all end all it may as well ossify abandoned

thread_tweets.text:
"the difference is that the product Bitcoin offers doesn't have many competitors"

Huh? There are hundreds of different types of store of value

If I want inflation protection I can buy SP500 or myriad other productive assets that have historically shown they can beat inflation 

If I want something that enables me to carry digital value  across borders fast, I can use stablecoins

If I want some doomsday scenario stash or hard money alternative, I can buy gold or silver

thread_tweets.text:
@kennyistyping @saylor "in comparison, almost no one cares about the mission of purely decentralized money any more (except maybe ETH)"

Well if no one cares about a problem, maybe the solution to that problem is not worth that much?
thread_tweets.full_text:
@kennyistyping @saylor "in comparison, almost no one cares about the mission of purely decentralized money any more (except maybe ETH)"

Well if no one cares about a problem, maybe the solution to that problem is not worth that much?

thread_tweets.text:
My point was it has competition in each particular problem it was supposed to solve

Ofc its combination of features makes it unique but its like saying

"My hawaiian-georgian fusion restaurant is very unique and has no competition in the whole city"

Well technically you are right, but in practice you are competing with every other restaurant too

My point was it has competition in each particular problem it was supposed to solve

Ofc its combination of features makes it unique but its like saying

"My hawaiian-georgian fusion restaurant is very unique and has no competition in the whole city"

Well technically you are right, but in practice you are competing with every other restaurant too

thread_tweets.text: @kennyistyping @saylor And now bitcoin is too big to compete for "lottery ticket bet / assymetric optionality upside" that was big feature of it when it was smaller
thread_tweets.full_text: @kennyistyping @saylor And now bitcoin is too big to compete for "lottery ticket bet / assymetric optionality upside" that was big feature of it when it was smaller

I am not sure what Bitcoin's guarantees of decentralisation even are tho and if market even cares, most people hold their BTC on exchanges or via ETF at this point

And surely it won't help that Bitcoin is resistant against change when security budget starts running out and quantum threat becomes more real in next 10 years

To me it still looks like a tech thats reaching mainstream top and is as likely its going to be replaced by a better one rather than some rock solid institution thats going to be relevant for next hundred years

thread_tweets.text: @kennyistyping @saylor I disagree on both points...not sure how useful it really it is and how to measure it and lindy is about time of existence of something so CDs at their peak were by definition more lindy than Bitcoin is now
thread_tweets.full_text: @kennyistyping @saylor I disagree on both points...not sure how useful it really it is and how to measure it and lindy is about time of existence of something so CDs at their peak were by definition more lindy than Bitcoin is now

tweets.full_text:
CD was a technology invented around 1985, hit its mainstream peak in 2000s and became obsolete before 2010

So 20-25 years overall

Bitcoin is a distributed network - an information technology that exists for 16 years since its inception, you can say its "mainstream" for last 2-3 years since we have ETFs

20 years is nothing in grand scheme of things, and concluding that Bitcoin is some rock solid thousand year old institution now is quite hubristic

"But you don't get it, there is 1 trillion USD already in Bitcoin, there are ETFs, pension funds are in, the institutions have vested interest to make it work"

Well SpaceX is 2T, NVDA 4T

As far as big guys are concerned they care only as long as it brings them fees and clients, when their clients stop showing appetite, the priorities will change fast

Bitcoin is still tech and tech world can moves on fast

Sure Bitcoin's decentralisation and resistance to change is one of its desired properties but if its be all end all it may as well ossify abandoned

"the difference is that the product Bitcoin offers doesn't have many competitors"

Huh? There are hundreds of different types of store of value

If I want inflation protection I can buy SP500 or myriad other productive assets that have historically shown they can beat inflation 

If I want something that enables me to carry digital value  across borders fast, I can use stablecoins

If I want some doomsday scenario stash or hard money alternative, I can buy gold or silver

@kennyistyping @saylor "in comparison, almost no one cares about the mission of purely decentralized money any more (except maybe ETH)"

Well if no one cares about a problem, maybe the solution to that problem is not worth that much?

My point was it has competition in each particular problem it was supposed to solve

Ofc its combination of features makes it unique but its like saying

"My hawaiian-georgian fusion restaurant is very unique and has no competition in the whole city"

Well technically you are right, but in practice you are competing with every other restaurant too

@kennyistyping @saylor And now bitcoin is too big to compete for "lottery ticket bet / assymetric optionality upside" that was big feature of it when it was smaller

I am not sure what Bitcoin's guarantees of decentralisation even are tho and if market even cares, most people hold their BTC on exchanges or via ETF at this point

And surely it won't help that Bitcoin is resistant against change when security budget starts running out and quantum threat becomes more real in next 10 years

To me it still looks like a tech thats reaching mainstream top and is as likely its going to be replaced by a better one rather than some rock solid institution thats going to be relevant for next hundred years

@kennyistyping @saylor rather than becoming*

Oh I don't criticise its inflexibility and agree its a feature

I criticise people like Saylor who think that 16 years of total history gives it any sort of staying power way into the future, its naive and hubristic given how other technologies completely disappeared from mainstream use after dominating for longer time period

@kennyistyping @saylor I disagree on both points...not sure how useful it really it is and how to measure it and lindy is about time of existence of something so CDs at their peak were by definition more lindy than Bitcoin is now

tweets.text:
Collorary: the whole EBI (exchange / broker / influencer) complex DOES NOT want you investing with a long term time horizon

They don't want you to buy and hold, they are incentivised by the fees and attention you pay

They want you to buy today and sell tomorrow, leverage long this and short that, chase the new narrative of the day, try to predict the next rotation

So the EBI complex is ALSO perfectly fine with tokens that don't accrue value and are good investments long term

They just need them to be volatile and capture headlines for a day
tweets.quoted_tweet_text:
most tokens in their current form are:

to a founder: cheap non dillutive capital

to a VC investor: free option for early liquidity

to a trader: volatility toy

to a long term holder: nightmare

tweets.text:
at this point every single frontier ai lab is trying to build their own ai chip to reduce dependency on nvidia

a weird consequence of this - chips that are hyper-specialised for a model:

openAI’s jalapeño chip + cerebras + nvidia stack = unique to GPT

anthropic’s custom chip + micron memory chip collab + tranium + google TPU = unique to Claude (chip agnostic)

i wonder if this ends up being the #1 differentiator between the top 5 labs
tweets.quoted_tweet_text: *SAMSUNG FOUNDRY IN TALKS WITH META ON A $6.54 BILLION CONTRACT FOR THIRD-GENERATION MTIA CHIPS (SEDAILY)
